critic_raw:
The methodology raises several interconnected concerns, organized by root cause.

## 1. Circular relationship between clustering and isotropy measurement

**Claim:** Applying PCA-based isotropy enhancement within clusters identified from the same embedding space produces a meaningful improvement over global methods.

**Why it might be wrong:** The clustering is performed in the original anisotropic embedding space. Anisotropy means the dominant principal components dominate distance calculations, so k-means or similar methods will partition points largely along those dominant directions. You are then removing exactly those directions within each partition. This creates a circularity: the clusters are defined by the anisotropic structure, and then the correction is applied to that same structure within those clusters. The result is that you may be measuring something closer to a within-cluster residual than a genuine local geometric structure. A global PCA followed by clustering on the residual would test whether the local structure is real or an artifact of the dominant modes.

**Evidence needed:** Compare cluster assignments before and after global isotropy correction. If cluster membership changes substantially post-global-correction, then the clusters were largely capturing the dominant anisotropic directions, not genuine semantic or syntactic locality. If cluster membership is stable, local structure is real.

## 2. Isotropy metric choice may favor the local method by construction

**Claim:** The local method produces a more isotropic space than global methods, as measured by standard isotropy metrics.

**Why it might be wrong:** The most common isotropy metric (partition function-based isotropy of Mu and Viswanath, or average cosine similarity) is computed globally over all embeddings. A local method that whitens within clusters will reduce within-cluster variance but may introduce between-cluster structure — clusters become separated spheres rather than a single anisotropic cone, but the global distribution could still be far from uniform on the sphere. The global metric may record an improvement simply because dominant within-cluster directions are removed, even though the between-cluster geometry is untouched or worsened. The method could score well on the metric while the practical geometry is not uniform.

**Evidence needed:** Report both within-cluster and between-cluster isotropy separately. Also check whether the inter-cluster angular distribution (directions from origin to cluster centroids) is itself isotropic. If cluster centroids remain in a cone, the global geometry is not fixed.

## 3. The downstream semantic task evaluation lacks a frozen-representation baseline

**Claim:** Post-processing improves semantic task performance, and the local method outperforms global post-processing.

**Why it might be wrong:** Semantic tasks with contextual embeddings are typically evaluated by training a light probe (logistic regression, MLP) on top of the fixed embeddings. When you apply a linear post-processing step (PCA whitening is linear), you are applying a linear transformation to the representation that the probe then has to work with. A linear probe on top of a linearly transformed embedding is equivalent to a linear probe with a different weight matrix on the original embedding — the expressivity is unchanged for linear probes. Any improvement is therefore attributable either to (a) reduced collinearity helping optimization of the probe, or (b) the post-processing discarding variance that the probe would have overfit to. Neither of these mechanisms is the one the paper argues for (i.e., 'better semantic structure'). If nonlinear probes are used, the argument changes, but then improvements may reflect fitting to post-processing artifacts.

**Evidence needed:** Report what probe architecture is used. If linear, explain why isotropy would help beyond conditioning. If nonlinear, show results with several depths to check for overfitting to post-processing artifacts.

## 4. Syntactic information removal is presented as a feature, not a bug, without ablation

**Claim:** Local post-processing removes syntactic information from contextual embeddings, and this is beneficial for semantic tasks.

**Why it might be wrong:** Many semantic tasks (STS, NLI, semantic role labeling) partially rely on syntactic structure. The claim that removing syntactic signal improves semantic performance is empirically testable but may hold only for shallow semantic similarity benchmarks (e.g., STS-B), where surface lexical overlap dominates and syntactic variation is noise. If the semantic tasks are chosen to be those where syntax is irrelevant, the paper is selecting evaluation conditions that favor its hypothesis. The claim would not generalize to semantic tasks that are syntactically demanding.

**Evidence needed:** Evaluate on tasks where syntax supports semantic disambiguation (e.g., semantic role labeling, coreference resolution). If performance degrades there but improves on STS-B, the story is not 'better semantics' but 'better lexical similarity at the cost of structural semantics.'

## 5. Number of clusters is a critical undocumented free parameter

**Claim:** The cluster-based method is a well-defined post-processing procedure.

**Why it might be wrong:** The number of clusters k is a free parameter that controls the granularity of the local correction. With k=1, the method collapses to global PCA whitening. With k=N (one point per cluster), the method does nothing. Somewhere in between, the method has maximum leverage. If k is tuned (even loosely) on downstream task performance or isotropy metrics computed on the same data used for evaluation, then the comparison against the global baseline is not fair — the local method has an extra degree of freedom. This is a standard hyperparameter leakage concern, but here it is particularly dangerous because isotropy metrics are computed on the same embeddings used for clustering.

**Evidence needed:** Report performance across a range of k values. Show that the selected k was not tuned on the evaluation split. Ideally, use a held-out validation set distinct from the downstream evaluation set to select k.

## 6. Silent misconfiguration risk: cluster-level PCA may silently degenerate when cluster sizes are small

**Claim:** PCA within each cluster captures meaningful dominant directions and removes them reliably.

**Why it might be wrong:** If cluster sizes are small relative to the embedding dimensionality (which is 768 for BERT-base), the sample covariance matrix within a cluster is rank-deficient. The estimated principal components are then artifacts of the small sample, not genuine dominant directions of the local distribution. The method may appear to work because it reduces empirical variance within clusters (guaranteed mathematically by removing estimated PCs), while the estimated PCs are noise vectors. On easy cases with large clusters this would produce plausible-looking results, masking the failure on small clusters.

**Evidence needed:** Report cluster size distribution. Report whether cluster-level covariance matrices are full-rank. Show that the number of removed PCs is strictly less than the minimum cluster size minus one.
all_issues_raised: Clustering is performed in the anisotropic embedding space, so clusters are defined by the dominant directions that PCA then removes — creating a circular artifact rather than capturing genuine local structure.
Global isotropy metrics may reward local whitening even when between-cluster geometry remains anisotropic, inflating the apparent improvement of the local method.
Downstream semantic task gains from a linear post-processing step are indistinguishable from probe conditioning effects, not improved semantic structure as claimed.
Syntactic information removal is positioned as beneficial without evaluating on tasks where syntax supports semantic understanding, selecting evaluation conditions that favor the hypothesis.
The number of clusters k is a free parameter that, if tuned on the same data used for evaluation, gives the local method an unfair advantage over the global baseline.

Small cluster sizes relative to embedding dimensionality make cluster-level PCA estimates unreliable, producing plausible-looking isotropy reductions that are mathematical artifacts of rank deficiency.
